figures.gloss: The incarnate Son of God, fully divine and fully human, who preached the Kingdom of God, was crucified under Pontius Pilate, and resurrected on the third day. He is the Logos through whom the universe was made, voluntarily entering history.
figures.facet: God does not merely send an avatar or a message; God becomes meat, suffers a humiliating, state-sanctioned execution, and validates the material world by resurrecting in a physical body, destroying the barrier between transcendent deity and human agony.
figures.provenance: The Canonical Gospels; Epistles of Paul; Nicene Creed.

figures.gloss: The foundational Christian mystery: one God existing eternally as three distinct, co-equal persons. They share a single divine essence but interact in dynamic, self-giving love, fundamentally defining the Christian understanding of the divine nature.
figures.facet: It shatters strict unitarian monotheism without collapsing into polytheism. God's inner life is intrinsically relational; love is not just something God does, but the ontological reality of what God is before anything was created.
figures.provenance: Gospel of Matthew; First Council of Nicaea; Athanasian Creed; Augustine's De Trinitate.

figures.gloss: The instrument of Roman torture transformed into the ultimate symbol of victory and reconciliation. Upon it, Christ bore the weight of human sin and death, offering himself as a spotless lamb to reconcile creation to the Creator.
figures.facet: The axis mundi is not a majestic world tree or cosmic mountain, but a bloody instrument of execution. Salvation is achieved through ultimate defeat, vulnerability, and the shedding of innocent blood, turning worldly power entirely upside down.
figures.provenance: Pauline Epistles; Gospel accounts of the Passion; Anselm's Cur Deus Homo.

figures.gloss: The young Jewish woman chosen to conceive the Son of God through the Holy Spirit. Venerated as the new Eve, her fiat ('let it be done') reversed the disobedience of Eden, making her the preeminent saint and intercessor.
figures.facet: Unlike primordial earth mothers who give birth to the cosmos through blind nature, her motherhood is a conscious, dangerous act of human consent to the divine will. The infinite God requires the permission of a teenage girl to enter history.
figures.provenance: Gospels of Luke and Matthew; Council of Ephesus (431 AD); Protoevangelium of James.

figures.gloss: The highest of angels who fell through pride, becoming the adversary of God and the deceiver of humanity. He tempts Christ in the desert, prowls the earth seeking souls to ruin, and is definitively defeated by the resurrection.
figures.facet: He is not a co-equal evil god in a dualistic war. He is a created, deeply limited being—a rebellious subordinate whose very attempts to destroy God's plan are ironically used by God to bring about ultimate redemption.
figures.provenance: Book of Revelation; Book of Job; Gospels; Augustine's City of God.

figures.gloss: The central sacrament of the Christian faith, instituted by Christ at the Last Supper. Bread and wine are consecrated to become the body and blood of Christ, offered as spiritual nourishment to the faithful.
figures.facet: It radically transforms ancient sacrificial meals. The deity is not just the recipient of the sacrifice but the sacrifice itself, voluntarily broken and consumed by the worshippers to incorporate them directly into the divine life.
figures.provenance: 1 Corinthians 11; The Didache; Justin Martyr's First Apology.

figures.gloss: The ancestral catastrophe in Eden where humanity rebelled against God's command, fracturing the original harmony of creation. This act infected all subsequent generations with a fallen nature, introducing death, labor, and spiritual estrangement from the Creator.
figures.facet: Death and suffering are not natural, intended features of the cosmos, nor the fault of a flawed demiurge. They are the direct, tragic consequence of human moral agency and the abuse of free will.
figures.provenance: Genesis 3; Romans 5; Augustine's anti-Pelagian writings.

figures.gloss: The eschatological hope that at the end of time, Christ will return to judge the living and the dead. Believers will be raised not as disembodied spirits, but in glorified, physical bodies to live in a renewed, physical heaven and earth.
figures.facet: It utterly rejects the Platonic and Gnostic idea that salvation is the soul escaping the prison of the body. The Christian endgame is the eternal redemption and glorification of the physical, material world.
figures.provenance: 1 Corinthians 15; Book of Revelation; Apostles' Creed.

figures.gloss: The third person of the Trinity, poured out on the apostles at Pentecost in tongues of fire; the Advocate (paraclete) promised by Christ who inspires scripture, distributes spiritual gifts, and indwells believers as the mode of God's continuing presence after the Ascension.
figures.facet: Divine presence here is not a memory of an absent founder but an ongoing, distributed indwelling — the same Spirit that moved over the waters of Genesis and spoke through the prophets now inhabits the ordinary believer's own body.
figures.provenance: Acts 2 (Pentecost); Gospel of John 14-16 (the Paraclete discourses); Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed (381 AD).

figures.gloss: The Galilean fisherman Simon, renamed Cephas/Peter ('Rock') by Jesus and given the keys of the kingdom; he denied Christ three times during the Passion, was restored by the risen Christ at the Sea of Tiberias, and became chief apostle of the early church, by tradition crucified in Rome under Nero.
figures.facet: His authority rests not on unbroken fidelity but on a failure publicly forgiven — the figure the papacy traces itself to is, by the gospels' own account, a man who first had to be restored from betrayal.
figures.provenance: Gospel of Matthew 16:18-19; Gospel of John 21:15-19; Acts of the Apostles; 1 Peter; Eusebius, Ecclesiastical History (on the Roman martyrdom tradition).

figures.gloss: A Pharisee and persecutor of the early church who, converted by a vision of the risen Christ on the road to Damascus, became its most consequential missionary and theologian; his epistles are the earliest surviving Christian writings and opened the faith to gentiles without requiring circumcision or Mosaic law.
figures.facet: Christianity's universalization beyond Judaism is the work of a convert who never knew the earthly Jesus, not a disciple who walked with him — the tradition's own expansion is founded on someone who met Christ only after the crucifixion.
figures.provenance: Acts of the Apostles 9, 13-28; the undisputed Pauline epistles (Romans, 1-2 Corinthians, Galatians, Philippians, 1 Thessalonians, Philemon).

figures.gloss: An ascetic desert prophet, son of the priest Zechariah, who preached repentance and baptized penitents in the Jordan, including Jesus; he identified Jesus as 'the Lamb of God' and was later beheaded on Herod Antipas's order at the request of Salome.
figures.facet: The gospel narrative gives its central rite an origin outside Jesus's own ministry — baptism begins as another man's practice, which Jesus submits to before beginning his own preaching.
figures.provenance: Gospels of Matthew 3, Mark 1, Luke 1 and 3, John 1; Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews 18.5.2.

figures.gloss: A Galilean follower of Jesus healed of 'seven demons,' present at the crucifixion and burial, and named in all four gospels as the first witness to the empty tomb and the risen Christ, who then commissioned her to announce it to the male apostles.
figures.facet: The resurrection the tradition treats as its hinge event reaches the church first through a woman's testimony, in a culture that did not count women as legal witnesses.
figures.provenance: Luke 8:2; Matthew 28, Mark 16, Luke 24, John 20 (resurrection appearance narratives).

figures.gloss: Commander of the heavenly host named in Daniel, Jude, and Revelation, where he leads the angels who cast the great dragon (Satan) down from heaven; venerated in popular devotion as patron of soldiers, police, and the dying, and as a guide of souls at the hour of death.
figures.facet: The chaoskampf here is fought by a subordinate, not the supreme God himself — combat with the dragon is delegated to a created being acting as heaven's field commander.
figures.provenance: Daniel 10, 12; Jude 1:9; Revelation 12:7-9; the Feast of St. Michael and All Angels (Michaelmas).

figures.gloss: The angel who announces to Zechariah the birth of John the Baptist and to Mary the conception of Jesus (the Annunciation), identifying himself as one who 'stands in the presence of God.'
figures.facet: The Incarnation is staged as a proposal requiring consent rather than a unilateral divine act — the angel delivers the offer and waits on Mary's answer before anything happens.

figures.gloss: The rite of water initiation performed in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, by which a person is washed of sin and incorporated into the church; read by Paul as a symbolic dying and rising with Christ.
figures.facet: Entry into the community is staged as death before it is staged as belonging — the initiate is submerged as if drowned before being raised as a member.
figures.provenance: Matthew 28:19; Romans 6:3-4; The Didache 7; Justin Martyr, First Apology 61.

figures.gloss: The reign of God breaking into history, announced by Jesus as already present in seed ('the kingdom of God is among you') yet not fully consummated until the end of the age; the central content of his preaching in the synoptic gospels, figured in parables of mustard seed, leaven, and hidden treasure.
figures.facet: Salvation is framed less as a place one goes after death than as a reign one enters now, already breaking into an unredeemed world through small, easily overlooked acts.
figures.provenance: Mark 1:15; Matthew 13 (Kingdom parables); Luke 17:20-21.

figures.gloss: The two-testament canon — the Hebrew scriptures received as the Old Testament and the twenty-seven books of the New Testament — fixed in broad outline by the late 4th century and treated as the normative written witness to God's revelation and the church's founding events.
figures.facet: The tradition roots its authority in a library assembled and closed by committee and council over centuries, not a single revealed book delivered whole to one prophet.
figures.provenance: Athanasius's 39th Festal Letter (367 AD); Synod of Hippo (393 AD) and Council of Carthage (397 AD).

figures.gloss: The doctrine that the living faithful, the dead awaiting resurrection, and the saints already glorified in heaven form one living body, bound together by prayer, intercession, and the veneration of relics.
figures.facet: Death does not remove a member from the community's active economy — the departed continue to act on behalf of the living, receiving petitions and dispensing aid, exactly as venerated ancestors do in traditions built around ongoing ancestral governance.
figures.provenance: Apostles' Creed; Hebrews 12:1 ('a great cloud of witnesses'); shared doctrinal articulation across the Catholic, Orthodox, and high-church Protestant traditions.

figures.gloss: The first man and woman formed by God in Eden, whose disobedience in eating the forbidden fruit — the Fall — is read as the origin of sin, labor, and death for all their descendants.
figures.facet: Every subsequent human inherits not a fresh moral slate but the consequence of two ancestors' single choice — original sin makes genealogy itself the vehicle of guilt, reversed only by a second, obedient 'Adam' and a second, consenting 'Eve.'
figures.provenance: Genesis 2-3; Romans 5:12-19; 1 Corinthians 15:22, 45.

figures.gloss: One of the twelve apostles, who betrayed Jesus to the Jewish authorities for thirty pieces of silver with a kiss in Gethsemane; Matthew records his suicide by hanging in remorse, while Acts preserves a differing tradition of his death.
figures.facet: The gospels place the betrayer inside the innermost circle, not outside it — the tradition's founding catastrophe is enacted by a chosen intimate, not an external enemy.
figures.provenance: Matthew 26-27; Mark 14; Luke 22; John 13, 18; Acts 1:18-19.

figures.gloss: The promised return of Christ in glory to judge the living and the dead, raise the bodies of the dead, and fully establish God's reign — the still-future consummation of the Kingdom already inaugurated at his first coming.
figures.facet: The tradition anchors its central hope in an unfulfilled future rather than a completed past event, staging the whole of church history as an interval of waiting.
figures.provenance: Matthew 24-25; 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18; 1 Corinthians 15:20-28; the Nicene Creed ('he will come again in glory').

edges.note: Christ is the second person of the Triune Godhead, co-eternal with the Father and the Spirit.
The cross is the defining altar upon which the incarnate God accomplishes the cosmic atonement.
Christ's perfect obedience and humility perfectly invert Satan's absolute pride and rebellion.
Mary is understood as the 'New Eve' whose obedient consent unties the knot of Eve's original disobedience.
The Eucharist is the perpetual, bloodless re-presentation of the bloody sacrifice accomplished once on the cross.
GOLD SEAM: Literal same-god identity; the specific Christian elaboration of the shared Abrahamic Messiah.
GOLD SEAM: Early Christian theology radically identified Jesus directly with Yahweh, applying Old Testament texts about the God of Israel to the crucified carpenter.
GOLD SEAM: Both are dying-and-rising figures whose resurrection conquers death and guarantees the eternal life of their followers.
GOLD SEAM: The iconography of Isis nursing Horus in Ptolemaic Egypt profoundly influenced early Christian visual depictions of the Madonna and Child.
GOLD SEAM: The cosmic adversary who seeks to ruin the supreme deity's good creation through deception, though Christian theology denies him co-eternal status.
GOLD SEAM: A cosmic tree upon which a divine figure hangs and suffers to gain ultimate victory and cosmic reconciliation.
GOLD SEAM: Both feature the ritual ingestion of wine as the blood of a suffering god, though the Eucharist transfigures ecstatic frenzy into a sober sacrament of communion.
GOLD SEAM: The eschatological vision of a final bodily resurrection and a remade, purified world entered Second Temple Judaism and early Christianity through Persian Zoroastrian contact.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme tempter who confronts the savior in the wilderness, offering worldly dominion to distract them from their world-saving mission.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions trace the flawed, difficult nature of the present world to a premature, rebellious act that fractured the creator's original design.
GOLD SEAM: The beloved, innocent son of the divine whose tragic, unjust death shifts the cosmic age and causes the whole world to weep.
GOLD SEAM: Spanish colonizers intentionally conflated the Mesoamerican lord of the dead with the Christian Devil, projecting moral evil onto a morally neutral cosmic function.
GOLD SEAM: In the Americas, indigenous populations and missionaries frequently synthesized the Christian cross directly with the Mesoamerican axis mundi.
GOLD SEAM: As Christianity displaced paganism, virgin goddesses like Athena were frequently overlaid with Marian devotion, most famously in the conversion of the Parthenon into a church of Mary.
GOLD SEAM: A structural resonance where ultimate reality manifests in three distinct but inseparable dimensions (Father/Dharmakaya, Son/Nirmanakaya, Spirit/Sambhogakaya).
GOLD SEAM: The Mass is the Roman elaboration of the shared Christian eucharist — same rite, same institution narrative, with transubstantiation as the distinctively Catholic metaphysical claim.
GOLD SEAM: The apparitions are Catholicism's living extension of the same Theotokos — the God-bearer of the Councils appearing as a localized, speaking presence.
GOLD SEAM: The Divine Liturgy is the Byzantine elaboration of the shared Christian eucharist — the same sacrament staged as realized eschatology.
GOLD SEAM: The same incarnate Christ of the shared tradition — the non-Chalcedonian family diverges at 451 over how, not whether, God and man are one in him.

GOLD SEAM: Direct descent — the Reformation is a reform within Latin Christianity, and Luther's theology of the cross re-centers the whole tradition on the Pauline atonement, claiming recovery, not new revelation.
GOLD SEAM: Same Christ, same creeds — Protestantism keeps Nicaea and Chalcedon intact and disputes only the mediating apparatus between Christ and the believer.

GOLD SEAM: Restorationist descent — the movement arose inside Second Great Awakening Protestantism and claims to restore, not reform, Christ's primitive church; the same Christ, radically reframed by an embodied Father and a non-Nicene Godhead.
GOLD SEAM: Two readings of the same Levitical premise that life resides in blood — the Eucharist centers on drinking the sacred blood, the Witness taboo forbids taking blood in at all.
Shaker doctrine reads Ann Lee as the second, female appearing of the Christ-spirit that first dwelt in Jesus — the two appearings completing the Father-Mother image of God.

GOLD SEAM: Divine Principle holds that Jesus won spiritual salvation but was crucified before marrying; the True Parents claim to complete the physical restoration he left undone — a doctrinal graft directly onto Christology.
The text's hinge is a rereading of Genesis 3 — the Fall as Lucifer's sexual seduction of Eve — making misused love the root sin and the restored God-centered family its cure.

Indemnity generalizes atonement: where the Cross pays once for all, tanggam distributes restitution across every believer's acts — restoration by accumulated conditions rather than a single sacrifice.
GOLD SEAM: the church's own name styles Kimbangu Christ's 'special envoy,' and his arrest, colonial trial, and thirty-year passion are read liturgically through the trial of Christ.
GOLD SEAM: Kimbanguist doctrine maps the founder and his three sons onto the Trinity — an attested, internally central, ecumenically contested re-inhabiting of Nicene structure.
GOLD SEAM: the same Jesus, with the deity subtracted — the INC honors Christ as savior and mediator while denying precisely the incarnation claim that defines Nicene Christianity.
GOLD SEAM: the INC defines itself against the Trinity as a post-apostolic corruption — the seam to Christianity runs through the doctrine it exists to refuse.
GOLD SEAM: the Rizalista churches explicitly identify Rizal with Christ — a Tagalog Christ whose December 30 martyrdom re-enacts the Passion on Philippine soil.

GOLD SEAM: Fang Bwiti's syncretic layer is attested history — mission Christianity (1900s-40s) folded into the liturgy, God and Christ absorbed under Fang names without displacing eboga; the missions fought it, the chapels kept both.

Both are eaten sacraments through which the divine becomes present in the communicant — but Bwiti stakes everything on direct vision where the eucharist works by faith; the banzie themselves draw the contrast.
In Christianized chapels the Sister of God is read alongside the Virgin — a feminine mediator before a remote godhead; the equation is reported unevenly across branches, so the seam stays faint.

GOLD SEAM: Rātana liturgy invokes Te Matua, Te Tama, Te Wairua Tapu — and with them ngā Anahera Pono, the Faithful Angels, and the Māngai himself; the mainstream churches broke with the movement in the mid-1920s over precisely this extension of Trinitarian prayer.

GOLD SEAM: a church built wholly of Christian scripture in Māori, received from the missions and re-covenanted — yet with its centre of gravity in Ihowa and the prophets of Israel's exile rather than in the missionaries' own churches, which Te Kooti's people refused.
Pasqually's Traite is an esoteric exegesis of Genesis: reintegration is the doctrine of the Fall run in reverse, restoring Adam's estate rather than atoning for his sin.
Documented transposition: Comte deliberately rebuilt Marian devotion — Madonna iconography, the Virgin-Mother utopia, a female moral providence — around Clotilde de Vaux and personified Humanity.
Comte modeled sociolatry's nine social sacraments explicitly on the Catholic sacramental system — the borrowing Huxley skewered as 'Catholicism minus Christianity' (1869).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the same Mary re-designated Ascended Lady Master and Archeia, her rosary devotion re-engineered into a decree practice.
The Spirit as the third co-equal person of the Godhead already named in the Trinity entry.
Named 'Rock' and given the keys of the kingdom (Matthew 16:18-19), then denies and is restored by him.
Converted by a vision of the risen Christ; his epistles are the earliest surviving Christological writings.
1 Corinthians 11:23-26 is the earliest surviving account of the Last Supper institution words.
Baptizes Jesus in the Jordan and names him 'the Lamb of God' before Jesus begins his own public ministry.
The rite's narrative origin is his own practice of baptizing penitents in the Jordan, prior to any Christian sacrament.
Named in all four gospels as the first witness to the risen Christ and the empty tomb.
Leads the angels who cast the rebellious Satan down from heaven in Revelation 12.
Delivers the Annunciation and awaits her consenting 'fiat' before the Incarnation proceeds.
The two dominical sacraments instituted directly by Christ in the gospel accounts, entry and ongoing nourishment.
The announced content of Jesus's own preaching in the synoptic gospels.
Paul names Christ the 'last Adam' whose obedience reverses the first Adam's disobedience (Romans 5; 1 Corinthians 15:45).
Patristic typology reads Mary as the 'New Eve' whose consenting fiat undoes Eve's original disobedience.
The first parents are the actors of the Fall narrative itself.
Both named apostles fail Christ during the Passion; the gospels record Peter's failure as wept over and forgiven, Judas's as final.
His betrayal sets in motion the arrest that leads directly to the crucifixion.
The still-future consummation of the reign Jesus announced as already breaking in.
Bodily resurrection of the dead is tied to Christ's return in Pauline eschatology (1 Corinthians 15; 1 Thessalonians 4).
Steiner's anthroposophy explicitly names its 'Spirit of the Age' after the Christian archangel, reworking the same figure as a planetary time-regent.
Trinidadian Orisha practice masks Ogún, the warrior-smith orisha, under the Catholic image of St. Michael the warrior archangel.
the same historical prophet of the Jordan, but Mandaeism inverts the Christian hierarchy, honoring Yahya as the true final prophet and rejecting Jesus.
Trinidadian Orisha practice masks Shango, the thunder-orisha, under the Catholic feast and image of St. John the Baptist.
the Christian Old Testament is the Hebrew Bible retained as scripture — direct textual inheritance, not analogy.
the Qur'an explicitly presents itself as confirming and correcting the Torah and the Gospel (Injil) that preceded it.
Jesus's kingdom preaching draws directly on Second Temple Jewish messianic and apocalyptic hope for God's coming reign.
the intercessory saint-economy this doctrine describes is the Christian-side mechanism that lets Trinidadian Orisha practice mask Shakpana under St. Francis.

both locate the source of moral disorder in an innate condition of human nature requiring active correction — Xunzi through ritual and teaching, Christianity through grace — rather than something drawn out by mere encouragement.
Santo Daime doctrine explicitly identifies the Queen of the Forest as the Virgin Mary — a Marian apparition relocated into the Amazon canopy.
A structural three-in-one completing worship in each system, though positivism's triad is cosmological and social (Humanity, Earth, Space) rather than three persons of one Godhead.
Her Only Begotten Daughter teaching directly mirrors and genders the only-begotten-Son Christology the movement otherwise extends through Moon.
His rejection of Nicene Trinitarianism, argued in print and paid for at the stake, is the doctrinal negation this tradition institutionalized.
the book takes over the Christian name and rebellion-narrative wholesale, but recasts the cosmic Adversary as a named, adjudicated political official rather than an eternal principle of evil.

(structural only): both traditions hang a primal human tragedy on a pair in a garden-like setting, but Urantia explicitly denies inherited sin — held at low tier against the temptation to over-claim shared theology.

Hungarian Catholic usage reads Boldogasszony ('the Blessed Woman') as simply Mary's title; folklore scholarship reads the same name as carrying forward a pre-Christian childbed goddess absorbed into it — the goddess side is a reconstruction, not independently attested, so filed a tier below the fully-attested Marian side.

Same antlered/goat-horned iconographic form claimed for opposite valence: Wicca's Murray-thesis history holds its Horned God as the very being Christianity demonized into the Devil, an identification Wicca asserts historically (on a now largely discredited witch-cult hypothesis) while rejecting it theologically.

The Guimar washed-up statue is documented (Espinosa, 1594) as identified with both the indigenous Sun Mother and, after conquest, with the Virgin of Candelaria, an advocation of Mary — one image, two theologies fused onto it.

Christian doctrine identifies the Trinity's first person directly with the covenant God of Israel while adding two co-equal persons — the same God of Abraham reorganized into a triune structure Judaism does not recognize.

Same person, opposite economy of access: early Friends held the Inner Light to BE Christ — 'the true Light which lighteth every man' (John 1:9) — present immediately and universally, where the source tradition locates Christ decisively in the incarnate, risen person mediated by church and sacrament.

Christian doctrine inherits and reworks the Hebrew Bible's Ha-Satan — the accusing officer of God's own court — into the fallen rebel-angel Devil, a documented theological transformation of the same prosecutor figure.

The Qur'an reworks the shared narrative of the angel commanded to bow before Adam who refused into a proud jinn merely permitted to tempt, not a fallen angel at cosmic war — same adversary-name, documented scriptural divergence.
Same name, opposite valence: Christianity's Lucifer is the fallen accuser cast from heaven; Luciferianism inverts him into a venerated liberator-god of self-deification.
Same name, opposite valence: Blavatsky rehabilitates Christianity's fallen adversary into a principle of awakened mind, naming her own magazine after him.
Same name, recast valence: Steiner's Lucifer is neither Christianity's condemned devil nor a hero but one of two necessary cosmic counter-forces to be balanced against Ahriman, not destroyed.
Same name, opposite valence: Christianity's fallen adversary becomes, in Stregheria, Diana's radiant brother-consort, the sun itself.
Same referent at opposite ontological standing: Christianity's cast-out cosmic enemy is, in theistic Satanism, a real, sentient being actively communed with and worshipped, not merely opposed.

Same name, incompatible kind: Christianity's literal fallen angel becomes, in the Satanic Temple, a non-theistic literary symbol of rebellion against arbitrary authority with no claimed supernatural existence.
Same name, incompatible kind: Christianity's literal fallen angel becomes, in LaVeyan Satanism, an egoist symbol of humanity's own carnal nature, not a worshipped entity.
Same third Person of the Nicene Trinity; Pentecostalism's spirit-baptism is a distinctively embodied, Azusa-era doctrine of that same Spirit's descent, not a different being.

ACIM keeps the Christian name and Trinity-adjacent function but re-metaphysicizes the Holy Spirit as the healed mind's inner Voice within an idealist cosmology where body and world are unreal — same name, sharply reworked ontology.

The name itself means 'holy spirit,' but Mandaeism's Ruha d-Qudsha is a demoness-queen of darkness ruling the planets — a deliberate polemical demotion of the Trinity's third person, not a coincidence of shared vocabulary.

dossier.essence: The global religion confessing Jesus of Nazareth as the Christ — the incarnate Son of God, crucified under Pontius Pilate around 30 CE and raised bodily from the dead. Beginning as a Jewish messianic movement, it became the largest religion on earth, roughly 2.3–2.4 billion people across Catholic, Orthodox, Protestant, and independent churches. Its central claim is that in this one execution and resurrection God reconciled the world to himself.
dossier.cosmos: One God, eternally triune (Father, Son, Holy Spirit), creates the world from nothing and good; humanity's primal rebellion (the Fall) introduces sin and death; God enters history as a particular human being, and the cross and resurrection accomplish redemption. History is linear and ends in Christ's return, the resurrection of the body, final judgment, and a renewed creation — not the soul's escape from matter but matter's glorification. Traditions differ on the interim state of the dead (immediate presence with God, purgation, or sleep until the resurrection), but heaven and hell mark the final alternatives.
dossier.practice: Entry by baptism; sustained by the Eucharist and weekly assembly on Sunday, the day of resurrection; ordered by a liturgical year climaxing at Easter. The ethical core is the double command — love of God and neighbor — elaborated through traditions of almsgiving, monasticism, and mission. Institutional forms span the episcopal hierarchies of Rome and the Orthodox East to congregational independency; sacramental counts and church order divide the families, while the Nicene Creed largely unites them.
dossier.canon: The Bible: the Old Testament (the Hebrew Scriptures, with deuterocanonical books in Catholic and Orthodox use) and the twenty-seven-book New Testament; the ecumenical creeds function as the canonical rule of interpretation.
dossier.reflects: Transcendence reconciled with history through one mediating person — the incarnational impulse, with victory located paradoxically in defeat.
sources: Jaroslav Pelikan, 'The Christian Tradition: A History of the Development of Doctrine', 5 vols. (1971–1989)
Diarmaid MacCulloch, 'Christianity: The First Three Thousand Years' (2009)
